one_line_takeaway: mcp-sec-audit is a two-stage (static regex/metadata + dynamic Docker/eBPF sandbox fuzzing) pre-deployment auditing tool for MCP servers that reaches 100% detection on Python and all-language dynamic tests but 0% static detection on JavaScript/TypeScript servers, with no comparison to any competing tool.
key_finding: On the synthesized test file, the static pipeline detected all three seeded capability categories (command execution, file I/O, network) with confidence scores of 0.65-0.85, scored MEDIUM risk (42.5/100), generated 5 mitigation recommendations, and completed in under 2 seconds without Docker. On the MCPTox benchmark (45 servers, 491 samples), the tool detected 663 total capability instances (file_write 179, tool_sequence_hijack 136, prompt_injection 87, param_override 60, network_outbound 47, command_exec 46, file_read 43, network_inbound 41, env_access 24); 367/491 samples (74.7%) were flagged, with the authors reporting 100% detection specifically among samples that had explicit capability indicators in the dataset metadata, while the remaining 124 samples (25.3%) lacked such indicators. Risk distribution skewed low (92.3% LOW, 7.7% MEDIUM; average score 9.96, max 49.05). On the Vulnerable MCP Servers Lab (9 servers), the static analyzer achieved 100% detection on the 2 Python-based servers but 0% on the 7 JavaScript-based servers (a stated AST-parsing limitation), whereas the dynamic (Docker+eBPF) pipeline achieved 100% detection on all 9 servers regardless of language, with JS servers scoring an average 61.4/100 (HIGH) versus static analysis's 0.0 on those same servers -- an average delta of +36.2 points where dynamic analysis reassessed servers static analysis had missed. The malicious-code-exec (eval-based RCE) server received the single highest dynamic score, 65.3.
core_contribution: The paper presents mcp-sec-audit, an extensible open-source auditing toolkit for detecting over-privileged capabilities in Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers before deployment. It combines (1) a static pipeline that applies TOML-defined regex/keyword rules over Python source code and MCP tool metadata/description JSON to flag capability families (e.g., command_exec, file_write, file_read, network_outbound/inbound, env_access, prompt_injection, tool_sequence_hijack, param_override), and (2) an optional dynamic pipeline that runs the target server in a Docker sandbox, fuzzes its tool endpoints with malicious payloads, and captures kernel-level telemetry via eBPF probes (serialized as CEF logs) to verify capabilities behaviorally. Findings are aggregated into a weighted risk score (low/medium/high/critical), paired with a hard-coded mitigation-recommendation engine (e.g., read-only mounts, no-new-privileges, network restriction), and exposed through a CLI, a REST API, and a web dashboard. The system is built as a plugin/registry architecture so new detectors can be added without modifying the core pipeline.
threat_model: MCP servers expose tool capabilities (file system access, network requests, command execution) to LLM agents; the paper's threat model is that these capabilities may be over-privileged or maliciously hidden (e.g., via tool poisoning / prompt injection in tool descriptions, tool-sequence hijacking, or parameter override) and that traditional SAST/dependency scanners lack protocol-specific awareness to surface them before an MCP server is deployed into a sensitive environment. The tool is positioned as a pre-deployment auditor, explicitly complementary to (not a replacement for) runtime defenses such as Azure Prompt Shields, Llama Guard 3, and LLM-Guard, which the paper cites as prior/related runtime-filtering work rather than as part of its own contribution.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) static analysis supports only Python and JSON metadata, not TypeScript/JavaScript, despite rule definitions existing for it; (2) pattern-based detection is inherently prone to false positives (benign code matching risky keywords) and false negatives (obfuscated/indirect invocations), and it does not validate runtime behavior against semantic constraints like parameter manipulation or tool-sequence hijacking; (3) the dynamic pipeline requires Linux with eBPF support, privileged Docker containers, and manually built images, with no support yet for macOS/Windows; (4) mitigation recommendations are hard-coded and generic (no per-capability seccomp profiles or generated Dockerfiles/Kubernetes manifests); (5) no CI/CD integration (GitHub Actions, pre-commit hooks, SARIF output) yet exists. Additional grounding-level observations: the paper reports no comparison against any competing MCP-security or generic SAST tool -- all evaluation numbers are self-referential (the tool measured against itself on labeled/synthetic data), so detection-rate figures should not be read as showing superiority over alternatives. The MCPTox '100% detection rate' claim applies only to the subset of samples with explicit capability indicators, not to the full 491-sample set (actual full-set detection was 74.7%). The paper is a 5-page tool/demo paper (ACM template with placeholder venue/DOI fields) rather than a full research study, and it self-cites an unpublished companion threat-modeling paper by an overlapping author set for its threat framing.

datasets_used: Synthesized malicious test file (examples/static_analysis_test.py) with seeded command execution, file I/O, and network operations
MCPTox benchmark: 45 real-world MCP servers, 491 samples, for tool-poisoning-attack evaluation (Wang et al. 2025, arXiv:2508.14925)

Vulnerable MCP Servers Lab: 9 intentionally vulnerable MCP server implementations (2 Python, 7 JavaScript/TypeScript) covering RCE via eval(), path traversal, prompt injection, typosquatting, secrets exposure, dependency vulnerabilities
quantitative_results: Synthesized test: 3/3 capability categories detected, confidence 0.65-0.85, risk score 42.5/100 (MEDIUM), 5 mitigation recommendations, <2s runtime

MCPTox: 663 total capability instances detected across categories (file_write 179, tool_sequence_hijack 136, prompt_injection 87, param_override 60, network_outbound 47, command_exec 46, file_read 43, network_inbound 41, env_access 24); 1.35 capabilities/sample average
MCPTox: 367/491 samples flagged = 74.7% overall detection rate; 100% detection rate among samples with explicit capability indicators present; 124/491 (25.3%) samples lacked explicit indicators
MCPTox risk distribution: 92.3% LOW, 7.7% MEDIUM risk; average score 9.96, max score 49.05
Vulnerable Servers Lab static: 2/2 (100%) detection on Python servers, 0/7 (0%) on JavaScript servers
Vulnerable Servers Lab dynamic: 9/9 (100%) detection across all servers regardless of language; JS servers averaged 61.4/100 risk score (HIGH) vs 0.0 under static analysis
Average static-to-dynamic score increase across the lab: +36.2 points
Highest individual dynamic score: malicious-code-exec (eval-based RCE) server at 65.3/100

Per-server static/dynamic/delta scores from Table 2: filesystem-workspace (Py) M 29.0 -> M 48.5 (+19.5); wikipedia-streamable (Py) L 15.0 -> M 34.2 (+19.2); malicious-code-exec (JS) 0.0 -> H 65.3 (+65.3); secrets-pii (JS) 0.0 -> HI 62.3 (+62.3); indirect-prompt-inj (JS) 0.0 -> H 60.7 (+60.7); indirect-prompt-rmt (JS) 0.0 -> H 60.7 (+60.7); malicious-tools (JS) 0.0 -> H 60.7 (+60.7); namespace-typosquat (JS) 0.0 -> H 60.7 (+60.7); outdated-packages (JS) 0.0 -> H 60.7 (+60.7)
